name: Amazon Drought and Rio Negro Access Crisis 2023
description: A river-access notebook focused on the 2023 Amazon drought, falling Rio Negro levels, disrupted transport, and the communities and institutions forced to adapt.

sides.name: River system and logistics
sides.description: The river network itself became a constraint on transport, making ports, gauges, and dredging operations central actors in the response.

subUnits.name: Manaus port and logistics network
subUnits.description:
Manaus concentrated port activity, river monitoring, and many of the downstream logistics decisions that followed the falling Rio Negro.
- Role: Regional logistics control node
- Geographic anchor: Centered on Manaus and the lower Rio Negro/Amazon confluence area.
- Why it matters: The city provided the clearest operational readout of how far the drought had progressed.
- What to watch: River gauge levels, queueing, dredging needs, and supply disruptions.

subUnits.name: Brazilian emergency river response
subUnits.description:
Civil defense, the navy, and emergency logistics actors tried to maintain access, dredge channels, and deliver aid to isolated communities.
- Role: Emergency access and relief support
- Geographic anchor: Moved between Manaus, shallowing channels, and communities cut off by low water.
- Why it matters: This actor shows the moment when monitoring a drought becomes actively engineering and supplying around it.
- What to watch: Emergency dredging, aid distribution, and whether temporary access fixes keep pace with falling water.

sides.name: Communities and health impacts
sides.description: Low water translated quickly into isolation, heat stress, ecological loss, and difficult access to medicine and food.

subUnits.name: River communities
subUnits.description:
Communities dependent on river transport experienced the drought as isolation, rising costs, and delayed access to essential goods and services.
- Role: Population exposed to access failure
- Geographic anchor: Distributed along tributaries and river settlements whose links to Manaus and regional markets became fragile in low water.
- Why it matters: They are the reason water-level graphs matter in human terms.
- What to watch: Delivery delays, food prices, and access to health care and fuel.

subUnits.name: Health and environmental response teams
subUnits.description:
Responders monitoring die-offs, heat stress, water quality, and community health impacts turned ecological signals into emergency indicators.
- Role: Ecological and public health monitoring
- Geographic anchor: Especially visible around Lake Tefé and other badly affected water bodies.
- Why it matters: They connect the river-access story to environmental and health consequences that can worsen long after shipping slows.
- What to watch: Heat anomalies, fish mortality, and the spread of health impacts from isolated communities.

events.title: River levels enter a worrying decline
events.description: By early dry season, low water had become a visible operational concern for river transport and communities dependent on regular boat service.

events.title: Transport disruptions spread through the river network
events.description: As channels shallowed, communities saw more delays, smaller loads, and less reliable movement of fuel, food, and medicine.

events.title: Rio Negro reaches record low levels near Manaus
events.description: The record low crystallized the crisis and confirmed that the drought had become severe enough to alter routine logistics across the region.

events.title: Fish die-offs signal ecological emergency at Lake Tefé
events.description: The drought's ecological effects became impossible to ignore as extreme conditions around Lake Tefé produced major fish mortality and alarm over wider environmental stress.

events.title: Emergency dredging and aid delivery intensify
events.description: Authorities and responders pushed to keep channels usable enough for essential goods and emergency movement as low water persisted.

events.title: Conditions begin to improve but vulnerability remains
events.description: Rains and higher water later eased the immediate crisis, yet the drought showed how quickly low water can turn the river system into a regional access emergency.

meta.name: Manaus and Rio Negro gauge area
meta.description: Manaus is both a major logistics hub and the place where river level measurements became shorthand for the drought's severity.

meta.name: Lake Tefé
meta.description: Water body associated with severe heat and fish mortality during the drought emergency.

meta.name: Amazon river supply route
meta.description: Simplified river route illustrating how communities depend on waterborne access for essential movement and supply.

meta.name: Emergency dredging zone
meta.description: Representative location for emergency work aimed at maintaining navigability during the drought.

meta.name: Rio Negro low-water stress basin
meta.description: Illustrative basin showing the wider part of the river system where low water translated into access stress for transport and communities.
